title: Cheviot Hills - Wellington Z1078 Wellington Memorial
crew_summary: All killed (returning from Hamburg bombing mission)
memorial_type: Crash site marker in Cheviot Hills
narrative: The Cheviot Hills, rising to 2,676 feet on the English-Scottish border, claimed 58 airmen's lives in 19 separate crashes during the Second World War. The combination of high terrain, unpredictable weather, and proximity to training airfields made this area particularly dangerous. One of the most well-documented incidents occurred on 15 January 1942 when Wellington Z1078 of 150 Squadron departed RAF Snaith in Yorkshire to attack Hamburg. On the return flight, the aircraft's navigation aids failed, causing it to drift far north over the North Sea. Lost and low on fuel, the Wellington struck a glancing blow on the lower slopes of The Cheviot at 1,500 feet, which was obscured by low snow clouds. The aircraft caught fire immediately. Observer Sgt Thomas Irving was killed on impact. Local farmer John Dagg of Dunsdale Farm heard the crash and made his way up the hill, guided by the smell of burning and exploding ammunition. He found three unconscious crew members near the burning wreckage and located two more sheltering nearby. Despite Dagg's heroic rescue, pilot Sgt Hunt and wireless operator Sgt Maple died from their injuries the following day. The heroism of Dagg and fellow shepherd Frank Moscrop inspired the creation of the collective memorial.
memorial_details: The Cheviot Airmen Memorial stands in the College Valley, opposite Cuddystone Hall. The original memorial was unveiled on 19 May 1995 by HRH The Duke of Gloucester, dedicated to the memory of all allied airmen who lost their lives in the Cheviot Hills during WW2. The monument is approximately 4 feet high with a sloping top, set inside a drystone circular wall designed to resemble a sheep stell (enclosure). The dedication in gilded Roman capitals is accompanied by RAF wings. In 2018, as part of RAF 100 centenary celebrations, a 25,000-pound project repaired and upgraded the memorial. A new polished granite and bronze monument was unveiled and rededicated on 6 September 2018, again by the Duke of Gloucester. The memorial is accessible via the College Valley road from Hethpool.

maintainer.notes: Crash site marker within Northumberland National Park.

external_narratives.summary: Detailed investigation record of Wellington Z1078 of 150 Squadron. Departed RAF Snaith to attack Hamburg on 15 January 1942; navigation aids failed on return causing the aircraft to drift north. Struck lower slopes of The Cheviot at 1,500 ft in snow clouds.
key_details: Navigation aids failed on return leg

external_narratives.summary: Crash site details for Wellington Z1078 on West Hill in the Cheviots. Documents the crash location, crew details, and the rescue by local farmer John Dagg of Dunsdale Farm who was guided by burning smell and exploding ammunition.
key_details: Three crew killed, three survived with injuries

external_narratives.summary: Walking route from Dunsdale to West Hill that passes the Wellington Z1078 crash site. Part of the National Park's guided walks highlighting wartime heritage in the Cheviot Hills.
